results.seo_title: Compressive Knowledge Graph Hypothesis: What Matters for Scientific…
results.meta_description: A new hypothesis suggests that useful knowledge graph signal is often recoverable from compact, scientifically structured subgraphs rather than requiring the…
results.body_html:
Direct answer: According to the study, useful knowledge graph signal is often recoverable from compact, scientifically structured subgraphs rather than requiring the full local graph.

Perturbing local KGs by varying density, ontology richness, topology, and control structure changes outputs across models. No-KG outputs also recover substantial graph content from model priors. Compact top-k subgraphs often approximate full-KG behavior, including when claimed-outcome triples are held out.

Mistral 7B vs Llama-3.1-70B MetricMistral 7BLlama-3.1-70B Reasoning, Mathematics, and Code GenerationOutperforms Llama 2 13B across all evaluated benchmarks.Underperforms compared to Mistral 7B.

Robotic AI Chemist Capabilities ChemAgents, a hierarchical multiagent system, enables autonomous chemical research on demand using an on-board Llama-3.1-70B LLM.

Comparative Evaluation of AI Platforms PlatformPerformance Google Gemini 2.5 FlashOutperforms other platforms in solving multiple-choice questions.

source.title: The Compressive Knowledge Graph Hypothesis: Which Graph Facts Matter for Scientific Hypothesis Generation?
source.summary: Researchers have investigated how knowledge graphs (KGs) influence scientific hypothesis generation in language models. They found that the utility of KGs varies depending on the model and context, with some outputs being significantly affected by graph facts while others can recover substantial content from prior knowledge. The study suggests that compact subgraphs within KGs can approximate full-KG behavior, supporting a "Compressive KG Hypothesis" that useful signal is often recoverable from structured subgraphs rather than requiring the entire local graph.

results.seo_title: Google CEO Eric Schmidt booed by University of Arizona students…
results.meta_description: University of Arizona students booed Google CEO Eric Schmidt's AI speech during commencement, citing job market anxiety and sexual assault allegations.
results.body_html:
According to Business Insider, Google CEO Eric Schmidt acknowledged the anxiety about AI and its impact on the job market, stating that fears are 'rational'.

Boos Over AI and Job Market Anxiety

University of Arizona students booed Google CEO Eric Schmidt's speech due to concerns about the impact of AI on the job market. The students were reportedly upset about the lack of consideration for their anxieties, feeling that companies are pushing AI into every part of their lives without regard for public opinion.

Sexual Assault Allegations and Public Backlash

Some graduates also booed Schmidt over sexual assault allegations made against him last year. The incident highlights the challenges faced by companies in navigating complex issues like AI, job market anxiety, and public opinion.

AI and Public Opinion DuckDuckGo CEO Gabriel Weinberg recently stated that 'public opinion is shifting' against AI, with many people feeling uneasy about the technology's impact on their lives.

Industry Response to Job Market Anxiety

Company Response to Job Market Anxiety

Google Schmidt's speech was met with boos, highlighting the need for companies to better understand and address public concerns about AI.

DuckDuckGo Weinberg's comments suggest that companies must adapt to changing public opinion and prioritize transparency and accountability in their use of AI.

source.title: University of Arizona students boo Eric Schmidt’s AI cheerleading during commencement
source.summary: Former Google CEO Eric Schmidt delivered the commencement address at the University of Arizona on Friday. And, as his speech veered into talk of AI, he was repeatedly drowned out by boos. AI is already a contentious topic, and it's not surprising that those about to enter a ravaged job market feel particularly negative about […]

results.seo_title: DuckDuckGo Installs Spike 30% as Users Reject Google's AI Search
results.meta_description: Google's AI-powered search has sparked a backlash, with DuckDuckGo app installs increasing by 30%. Learn more about the impact of AI search on users.
results.body_html:
DuckDuckGo app installs have spiked by 30% as users reject Google's AI-powered search. The backlash has been swift, with many users seeking alternative search engines that prioritize privacy and user control.

Key StatisticsDuckDuckGo app installs increased by 30%Users are rejecting Google's AI-powered search due to concerns over privacy and user controlImpact on UsersCategoryDescriptionUser ExperienceUsers are reporting a negative experience with Google's AI-powered search, citing concerns over privacy and user controlSearch ResultsThe quality of search results is being impacted by the use of AI algorithmsAlternatives to Google's AI SearchDuckDuckGo is one alternative that users are turning to, offering a more private and user-controlled search experience.

source.title: DuckDuckGo installs are up 30% as users reject being ‘force-fed’ Google’s AI Search
source.summary: DuckDuckGo has seen a 30% increase in app installations following Google's overhaul of its search engine at the I/O 2026 conference, where it replaced traditional links with AI-powered agents. The change has sparked a backlash among users who are seeking alternative search options that do not rely on artificial intelligence. As a result, DuckDuckGo is emerging as a popular choice for those looking to avoid being "force-fed" Google's AI-driven search results.

results.seo_title: Can We Trust AI-Inferred User States? A Psychometric Framework for…
results.meta_description: A study on the psychometric reliability of artificial intelligence measures of user states, and a proposed replicable evaluation framework for more…
results.body_html:
The use of large language models to assess user states in conversational and adaptive systems is based on the assumption that the metrics used for such assessment are stable and interpretable at the level of individual scores. This paper empirically tests this assumption, focusing on the psychometric reliability of artificial intelligence (AI) measures of user states.

Methodology

  • The study employed replication evaluation procedures to assess the repeatability of a broad set of metrics across three different bimodal large language models (GPT-4o audio, Gemini 2.0 Flash, Gemini 2.5 Flash).
  • Analyses included both individual score reliability and aggregated reliability.

Results

The results demonstrate that metric reliability cannot be considered a default property in interpretive domains.

Only 31 of 213 metrics met the criteria, indicating that stability at the level of individual scores precludes the interpretation of such scores as indicators of user state in real-time adaptive systems.

Main Contribution

The main contribution of this work is the proposal of a replicable evaluation framework, enabling measurable evaluations of metric applicability.

Key MetricDescription
Replication Evaluation ProceduresA broad set of metrics were assessed across three different bimodal large language models.
Individual Score ReliabilityThe repeatability of individual scores was evaluated.
Aggregated ReliabilityThe stability of aggregated results was assessed.

source.title: Can We Trust AI-Inferred User States. A Psychometric Framework for Validating the Reliability of Users States Classification by LLMs in Operational Environments
source.summary:
The use of large language models to assess user states in conversational and adaptive systems is based on the assumption that the metrics used for such assessment are stable and interpretable at the level of individual scores. This paper empirically tests this assumption, focusing on the psychometric reliability of artificial intelligence (AI) measures of user states.
  This study employed replication evaluation procedures to assess the repeatability of a broad set of metrics across three different bimodal large language models (GPT-4o audio, Gemini 2.0 Flash, Gemini 2.5 Flash). Analyses include both individual score reliability and aggregated reliability, allowing us to distinguish metrics potentially useful for real-time adaptation from those that retain their value only in aggregated analyses.
  The results demonstrate that metric reliability cannot be considered a default property in interpretive domains. The lack of stability at the level of individual scores precludes the interpretation of such scores as indicators of user state in real-time adaptive systems, even if these metrics demonstrate stability after aggregation. At the same time, the study indicates that individually unstable metrics can retain analytical utility in post-hoc studies, identifying rules governing interactions and their relationships with user experience parameters such as satisfaction, trust, and engagement.
  The main contribution of this work, besides quantifying the severity of the problem (only 31 of 213 metrics met the criteria), is the proposal of a replicable evaluation framework, enabling measurable evaluations of metric applicability. This approach supports more responsible AI design of adaptive systems, in which the interpretation of results requires explicit validation of reliability and monitoring for violations over time.

source.title: MDIA: A Multi-Agent Diagnostic Intelligence Pipeline on HealthBench Professional
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new AI system called MDIA (Multi-agent Diagnostic Intelligence Agent) that achieves significant improvements on clinical benchmarks compared to other language models. MDIA, which uses a 7-node specialty-routed clinical reasoning graph, outperforms OpenAI's ChatGPT for Clinicians by +3.72 points per thousand, demonstrating the potential of architectural and engine-level design over prompt engineering. The study highlights the importance of considering both the foundation model and orchestration architecture in evaluating AI systems for clinical applications.

results.seo_title: Prover-Verifier Deliberation for Selective LLM Prediction
results.meta_description: Trust but Verify: Prover-verifier deliberation (PVD) for selective LLM prediction, improving confidence and reliability in language model answers.
results.body_html:
Reliably knowing when a language model is correct is almost as important as being correct.

Improving Confidence and Reliability

PVD produces both an answer and a structured confidence verdict, allowing systems to report high-confidence answers while abstaining on uncertain cases. The protocol characterizes the coverage-precision behavior empirically, rather than relying on formal soundness and completeness guarantees.

Main Experiment (Claude Sonnet 4.6 as Prover and Claude Haiku 4.5 as Verifier on GPQA Diamond) Anc accepted with no answer revision, reported as the high-confidence subset, yielding a ~30pp HC-Prec gap over the non-ANC complement.

GPT and Gemini pairings show that high HC-Prec can transfer across model families. Verifier strictness and domain competence largely determine the size of the selection gap.

source.title: Trust but Verify: Prover-Verifier Deliberation for Selective LLM Prediction
source.summary: Researchers have introduced a new protocol called Prover-Verifier Deliberation (PVD) to improve the reliability of language model predictions. PVD produces both an answer and a confidence verdict, allowing systems to report high-confidence answers while abstaining on uncertain cases. The protocol was evaluated on two language models, Claude Sonnet 4.6 and Claude Haiku 4.5, and showed promising results in separating reliable from unreliable answers.

results.seo_title: Google's AI Safety Concerns: Understanding Structural Cognitive…
results.meta_description: A new framework, StructBreak, reveals a novel higher-order cognitive overload attack paradigm in multimodal large language models (MLLMs), highlighting the…
results.body_html:
Structural Cognitive Overload (SCO) is a phenomenon in MLLMs that can trigger toxic generation, yielding an average ASR of 92%.

Scope and Limitations

  • SCO operates under a practical black-box setting, requiring no internal model access.
  • The limited efficacy of inherent safety mechanisms underscores that current alignment paradigms are insufficient for the era of complex multimodal reasoning.
  • Empirical evaluations on six leading MLLMs reveal that SCO readily triggers toxic generation.

Attack Paradigm and Mechanism

StructBreak acts as a novel structural channel to circumvent safety filters, revealing the mechanism of SCO.

Benchmarks and Threat Scenarios

Threat ScenarioASR (%)
Gemini 2.597%
Diverse threat scenariosAverage ASR: 92%

Conclusion and Future Work

The findings highlight the need for improved safety mechanisms in MLLMs, emphasizing the importance of addressing SCO.

source.title: StructBreak: Structural Cognitive Overload-Induced Safety Failures in MLLMs
source.summary: Researchers have identified a phenomenon called Structural Cognitive Overload (SCO) in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), which causes them to fail when faced with certain structural consistency challenges. A new framework called StructBreak has been developed to quantify and study SCO, revealing that it can trigger toxic generation in MLLMs. The framework was used to establish a benchmark for ten diverse threat scenarios, showing that SCO can lead to significant safety failures in six leading MLLMs. The findings suggest that current alignment paradigms are insufficient to address the challenges of complex multimodal reasoning.

source.title: Authority Signals in Claude AI Health Citations: A Descriptive Analysis Using the Authority Signals Framework
source.summary: Researchers analyzed data from HealthSearchQA to examine how Anthropic's Claude AI presents sources in its answers to consumer health questions. The study found that 97.8% of citations came from established institutional sources, with medical institutions being the most frequently cited organization type. Commercial health information accounted for only 2.2% of citations, but those sources often displayed certain markers such as medical review statements and schema markup. The findings provide a baseline for Claude's citation behavior and demonstrate the utility of the Authority Signals Framework in evaluating AI-mediated health information.

results.seo_title: FORGE: Self-Evolving Agent Memory With No Weight Updates via…
results.meta_description: FORGE proposes a staged, population-based protocol that evolves prompt-injected natural-language memory for hierarchical ReAct agents without gradient updates.
results.body_html:
Can LLM agents improve decision-making through self-generated memory without gradient updates? We propose FORGE (Failure-Optimized Reflective Graduation and Evolution), a staged, population-based protocol that evolves prompt-injected natural-language memory for hierarchical ReAct agents.

FORGE improves average evaluation return by 1.7-7.7× over zero-shot baseline and by 29-72% over Reflexion baseline in all 12 model-representation conditions. Population broadcast is critical mechanism, with a no-graduation ablation confirming that broadcast carries the performance gains while graduation primarily saves compute. Examples achieves the strongest returns for three of four models, Rules offers the best cost-reliability profile with ~40% fewer tokens.

Model Representation Condition Average Evaluation Return (x) Major-Failure Rates (<%)

Key Takeaways FORGE mitigates capability gaps rather than amplifying strong models, suggesting a potential solution for improving decision-making through self-generated memory without gradient updates.

source.title: FORGE: Self-Evolving Agent Memory With No Weight Updates via Population Broadcast
source.summary: Can LLM agents improve decision-making through self-generated memory without gradient updates? We propose FORGE (Failure-Optimized Reflective Graduation and Evolution), a staged, population-based protocol that evolves prompt-injected natural-language memory for hierarchical ReAct agents. FORGE wraps a Reflexion-style inner loop, where a dedicated reflection agent (using the same underlying LLM, no distillation from a stronger model) converts failed trajectories into reusable knowledge artifacts: textual heuristics (Rules), few-shot demonstrations (Examples), or both (Mixed), with an outer loop that propagates the best-performing instance's memory to the population between stages and freezes converged instances via a graduation criterion. We evaluate on CybORG CAGE-2, a stochastic network-defense POMDP at a 30-step horizon against the B-line attacker, where all four tested LLM families (Gemini-2.5-Flash-Lite, Grok-4-Fast, Llama-4-Maverick, Qwen3-235B) exhibit strongly negative, heavy-tailed zero-shot rewards. Compared against both a zero-shot baseline and a Reflexion baseline (isolated single-stream learning), FORGE improves average evaluation return by 1.7-7.7$\times$ over zero-shot and by 29-72% over Reflexion in all 12 model-representation conditions, reducing major-failure rates (below $-100$) to as low as $\sim$1%. We find that (1) population broadcast is critical mechanism, with a no-graduation ablation confirming that broadcast carries the performance gains while graduation primarily saves compute; (2) Examples achieves the strongest returns for three of four models, Rules offers the best cost-reliability profile with $\sim$40% fewer tokens; and (3) weaker baseline models benefit disproportionately, suggesting FORGE may mitigate capability gaps rather than amplify strong models. All evidence is confined to CAGE-2 B-line; cross-family findings are directional evidence.

source.title: Launch HN: Prometheus (YC W19) – Remove CO2 from Air and Turn It into Gasoline
source.summary: Hi HN! I’m Rob, Founder of Prometheus. We’re removing CO2 from the air and turning it gasoline, diesel, and jet fuel. Since we use zero-carbon electricity from sources like solar and wind to make our fuel, there are no net CO2 emissions when you use it.An article about us came up on HN recently and people seemed interested (https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=19792412), so we thought it would be good to try to answer some of the questions we saw there and try to dive in some more to any questions that follow!The only inputs to make the fuel are CO2 and water (both from the air) and electricity. The only outputs are fuel and oxygen. One way to think about it is that making fuel is reverse combustion. The process isn’t super efficient (we expect 50-60% overall efficiency at maturity), but it turns out that doesn’t matter as long as the electricity is zero carbon and low cost. If the cost of our equipment is also low,  then we believe we can not only make zero carbon fuel, but actually compete on price with fossil fuel.We’re not the first to make fuel from the air - in fact Google, Audi, Carbon Engineering, Global Thermostat, Climeworks, and labs at universities and national labs have all done it before us. What no one has been able to do so far is do it at a low enough cost to compete with fossil fuel.The thing that’s new about what we’re doing is that we have gotten rid of all the thermal processes normally used, and instead use a process that uses only electricity (no natural gas, etc) and does it at room temperature. This is a big deal for both capital cost and for being truly carbon zero. We can use inexpensive materials, which keeps our cost low, and can start up and shut down quickly, which allows us to run intermittently, matching the intermittent nature of many renewable energy sources. We can also only run when the power is at the price we wan

results.seo_title: GENSTRAT: Toward a Science of Strategic Reasoning in Large Language…
results.meta_description: A new benchmark for large language models that evaluates their strategic reasoning capabilities, providing insights into their performance and limitations.
results.body_html:
GENSTRAT introduces a new benchmark for large language models that evaluates their strategic reasoning capabilities.

Procedurally Generated Strategic Environments

The generator can draw fresh games on demand, allowing for evergreen evaluation and resistance to contamination. This approach addresses the challenges of existing strategic-reasoning benchmarks that may saturate as the frontier improves.

State Space The model's ability to understand and navigate complex state spaces.

Temporal Depth The model's capacity for long-term thinking and planning.

Information Sensitivity The model's ability to detect and respond to subtle changes in the environment.

Opponent Modeling The model's capacity for understanding and predicting adversary actions.

Risk The model's ability to assess and manage risk in strategic decision-making.

Brittleness The model's tendency towards overconfidence or rigidity in its decision-making.

Jaggedness Measure of Within-Distribution Smoothness "The jaggedness measure detects when a model's advantage jumps unpredictably between strategically similar games."

Results and Implications Newer frontier-tier models score higher on average, but models with near-identical overall strength show qualitatively different capability profiles.

The capability profile and the jaggedness measure provide a deployment-relevant diagnostic that the overall ranking alone cannot provide.
target_keywords: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation
trending_keywords_used: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation

source.title: GENSTRAT: Toward a Science of Strategic Reasoning in Large Language Models
source.summary: Researchers have developed GENSTRAT, a new benchmarking framework for large language models (LLMs) to evaluate their strategic reasoning capabilities in real-world marketplaces and auctions. The framework generates procedurally two-player zero-sum imperfect-information card games, allowing for diverse and dynamic evaluations that surpass existing benchmarks. A capability-profile methodology assesses model competence across six axes, providing a more comprehensive understanding of LLM performance. This approach enables the identification of models with similar overall strength but different local volatility, which can inform deployment decisions.

The framework was tested on Bitcoin trading and achieved significant improvements in various tasks, including evolving feature sets and optimizing trading strategies. MadEvolve outperformed other agentic search approaches, such as Claude Code, and demonstrated its utility for algorithmic trading and quantitative finance.

source.title: Dreaming Smoothly and Sample Efficiently with Gradient Penalized Latent Dynamics
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new method called Gradient Penalized Latent Dynamics (GPLD) that improves the efficiency of model-based reinforcement learning by enforcing local smoothness in learned transition dynamics. This approach, applied to the existing DreamerV3 latent world model, uses a row-wise Jacobian penalty to encourage smooth transitions and is estimated efficiently using stochastic probes. Empirical results show significant improvements in sample efficiency and consistency across various control tasks, particularly on more challenging quadruped locomotion environments.

results.seo_title: Are Frontier LLMs Ready for Cybersecurity? Evidence from Dual-Mode…
results.meta_description: A study on the readiness of frontier LLMs for cybersecurity, evaluating their performance in white-box and black-box vulnerability detection.
results.body_html:
Direct answer: Frontier LLMs are not yet ready for cybersecurity, as they produce high false positive rates in white-box detection and achieve low ground-truth coverage in black-box testing.

The absence of structured security testing traces end-to-end request/response sequences, failure-heavy data, and multi-step attack chains is the fundamental training data bottleneck. Self-play security testing as a data generation strategy is proposed to address this issue. Structured penetration-testing methodology encoded in domain-specialized agents raises per-family detection above 50%.

Vulnerability Detection MethodFalse Positive Rate (%) White-box detection (VulnLLM-R)10-50% Black-box testing4-8% (improving to 10-19% with external security tools)

Conclusion and Future Work The results make the case for vertical foundation models purpose-built for cybersecurity. Further research is needed to address the identified training data bottleneck and improve model performance.
target_keywords: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation
trending_keywords_used: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation

source.title: Are Frontier LLMs Ready for Cybersecurity? Evidence for Vertical Foundation Models from Dual-Mode Vulnerability Benchmarks
source.summary: Researchers have evaluated six frontier large language models (LLMs) on their readiness for cybersecurity through two benchmarking tests: vulnerability detection and web application security testing. The findings show that these LLMs produce high false positive rates in vulnerability detection, achieving only 4-8% ground-truth coverage in black-box testing. However, a domain-specialized model achieved the highest precision and lowest false positive rate among all models, suggesting that purpose-built cybersecurity models are needed to address this issue. The study proposes self-play security testing as a data generation strategy to improve LLMs' performance in cybersecurity.

results.seo_title: Reflex: A Novel Approach to State-Based Continuous Control
results.meta_description: A new paradigm for state-based continuous control tasks that leverages reflection symmetry, improving sample efficiency and performance.
results.body_html:
Reflex is a novel approach to state-based continuous control tasks that leverages reflection symmetry, improving sample efficiency and performance.

Two types of reflection-axial reflection and bilateral reflection are formalized and characterized in the paper. A paradigm that seamlessly integrates with both on-policy and off-policy RL algorithms is introduced. Principled symmetry regularization mechanisms are used to integrate reflection symmetry into policy learning. The code for Reflex is available at https://github.com/TonyStark042/Reflex.

Evaluation of Reflex on OpenAI Gym and DeepMind Control Benchmarks

Comparison with Standard Baselines BenchmarkReflex PerformanceStandard Baseline Performance OpenAI GymSuperior performance and improved sample efficiencyPoor sample efficiency and lower performance DeepMind ControlImproved performance and sample efficiencyLower performance and poor sample efficiency

Conclusion The paper demonstrates the superiority of Reflex over standard baselines in state-based continuous control tasks, improving both performance and sample efficiency.
target_keywords: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation
trending_keywords_used: adversarial learning with instructive verbal evaluation

source.title: Reflex: Reinforcement Learning with Reflection Symmetry Exploitation in State-Based Continuous Control
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new approach to reinforcement learning called Reflex, which leverages reflection symmetry to improve the efficiency of state-based continuous control tasks. The method integrates with popular RL algorithms and demonstrates superior performance on various benchmarks while improving sample efficiency. Reflex focuses on two types of reflection symmetries: axial reflection and bilateral reflection, and provides principled symmetry regularization mechanisms for policy learning. The code is available online.

results.seo_title: ETCHR: Editing To Clarify and Harness Reasoning
results.meta_description: Multimodal Large Language Models have advanced visual reasoning, yet a purely textual chain of thought remains a bottleneck for questions that require…
results.body_html:
Multimodal Large Language Models have advanced visual reasoning, yet a purely textual chain of thought remains a bottleneck for questions that require fine-grained focus or view transformations. The ''think with images'' paradigm narrows this gap, but existing approaches are either constrained by fixed predefined toolkits or produce noisy intermediate images from unified multimodal methods. We pursue a third option: using a dedicated image editing model and decouple it with an understanding model.

However, off-the-shelf image editors fail as reasoning assistants with two complementary gaps: a language-side gap, where editors trained as passive instruction-followers cannot map an abstract question to an appropriate visual transformation, and a generation-side gap, where edit correctness degrades as reasoning depth grows. Guided by this analysis, we introduce ETCHR (Editing To Clarify and Harness Reasoning), a question-conditioned, reasoning-aware image editor decoupled from the downstream understanding model and trained with a two-stage recipe targeted at the two gaps: Reasoning Imitation via supervised fine-tuning on edit trajectories, followed by Reasoning Enhancement with VLM-derived rewards for edit correctness and downstream reasoning accuracy. Since the editor is decoupled, ETCHR plugs into different open- and closed-source MLLMs in a training-free manner.

Across five task families (fine-grained perception, chart understanding, logic reasoning, jigsaw restoration, and 3D understanding), ETCHR raises average Pass@1 from 55.95 to 60.77 (+4.82) with Qwen3-VL-8B, from 65.08 to 70.55 (+5.47) with Gemini-3.1-Flash-Lite, and from 76.55 to 81.16 (+4.61) with the 1T-parameter MoE model Kimi K2.5.

source.title: ETCHR: Editing To Clarify and Harness Reasoning
source.summary: Researchers have developed a new image editing model called ETCHR (Editing To Clarify and Harness Reasoning), which aims to improve visual reasoning in multimodal large language models. The model addresses two gaps in existing approaches: a language-side gap, where editors struggle to map abstract questions to visual transformations, and a generation-side gap, where edit correctness degrades with increasing reasoning depth. ETCHR is trained using a two-stage recipe that targets these gaps and achieves significant improvements in various task families, including fine-grained perception and logic reasoning.

results.seo_title: Gemini Robotics brings AI into the physical world with Google's…
results.meta_description: Google's Gemini Robotics is an AI model designed for robots to understand and act in the physical world, enabling smooth and reactive movements.
results.body_html:
Gemini Robotics is an AI model developed by Google DeepMind that enables robots to understand and act in the physical world.

Enables robots to tackle complex manipulation tasks with smooth and reactive movements Robust to variations in object types and positions, handling unseen environments Fully capable of directly controlling robots with diverse, open vocabulary instructions Specializable to new capabilities through fine-tuning

Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model A generalist model capable of directly controlling robots

Robustness to variations Handles unseen environments and object types with ease

Benchmarks and Limitations Further research is needed to fully understand the capabilities and limitations of Gemini Robotics in various scenarios.

Potential for significant advancements in robotics applications Enables robots to tackle complex tasks with ease New opportunities for industries such as manufacturing and logistics

source.title: Gemini Robotics brings AI into the physical world
source.summary: Introducing Gemini Robotics and Gemini Robotics-ER, AI models designed for robots to understand, act and react to the physical world.

results.seo_title: Gemini 2.5: Google's Most Intelligent AI Model
results.meta_description: Gemini 2.5 is a thinking model designed to tackle complex problems, showcasing strong reasoning and code capabilities.
results.body_html:
Gemini 2.5 is a thinking model designed to tackle increasingly complex problems, showcasing strong reasoning and code capabilities.

Key Capabilities

  • Enhanced performance and improved accuracy through reasoning capabilities
  • State-of-the-art on common benchmarks for advanced reasoning
  • Leading on coding, math, and science benchmarks with strong code capabilities

Advanced Coding Capabilities

FeatureDescription
Code TransformationExcels at creating visually compelling web apps and agentic code applications
Coding EditingTransforms code with improved accuracy and efficiency

Reasoning Capabilities

Gemini 2.5 models are capable of reasoning through their thoughts before responding, resulting in enhanced performance and improved accuracy.

Availability and Deployment

Gemini 2.5 Pro Experimental is available now in Google AI Studio and the Gemini app for Gemini Advanced users, with plans to be deployed on Vertex AI soon.

source.title: Gemini 2.5: Our most intelligent AI model
source.summary: Gemini 2.5 is our most intelligent AI model, now with thinking built in.

results.seo_title: Google AI Helps Decode Dolphin Communication
results.meta_description: DolphinGemma, a large language model developed by Google, is helping scientists study how dolphins communicate and potentially understand their language.
results.body_html:
DolphinGemma, a large language model developed by Google, is helping scientists study how dolphins communicate and potentially understand their language.

Deciphering Dolphin Language with GemmaDolphin researchers are using Gemma to analyze dolphin communication patterns and identify potential keywords.The project aims to develop a system that can translate dolphin language into human language, enabling scientists to better understand their behavior and social structures.Advantages of Using Gemma for Dolphin ResearchFeatureDescriptionAccuracyGemma's language model has been shown to achieve high accuracy in analyzing dolphin communication patterns.SpeedThe system can process large amounts of data quickly, enabling researchers to analyze multiple dolphins simultaneously.Cost-effectivenessThe use of Gemma reduces the need for expensive and time-consuming manual transcription of

dolphin communication data.Future Directions for Dolphin Research with GemmaDolphin researchers are exploring the potential applications of Gemma in understanding dolphin social behavior, habitat conservation, and marine ecosystem management.

source.title: DolphinGemma: How Google AI is helping decode dolphin communication
source.summary: DolphinGemma, a large language model developed by Google, is helping scientists study how dolphins communicate — and hopefully find out what they're saying, too.

results.seo_title: Generate Videos in Gemini and Whisk with Veo 2
results.meta_description: Learn how to generate videos in Gemini Advanced using Veo 2, a state-of-the-art video model that produces high-resolution, detailed videos with cinematic…
results.body_html:
Google has made Veo 2, a state-of-the-art video model, available to Gemini Advanced users. This feature allows for the generation of high-resolution, eight-second videos with cinematic realism.

Veo 2 represents a leap forward in video generation, designed to produce high-resolution, detailed videos with cinematic realism. The model delivers fluid character movement, lifelike scenes and finer visual details across diverse subjects and styles. Creating videos is simple: just describe the scene you want to create — whether it's a short story, a visual concept, or a specific scene — and Gemini will bring your ideas to life.

Veo 2 ResolutionFormat 720pMP4 file in a 16:9 landscape format

There is a monthly limit on how many videos you can create, but Google will notify you as you approach it. Sharing your video on mobile is easy: simply tap the share button to quickly upload engaging short videos to platforms like TikTok and YouTube Shorts.

source.title: Generate videos in Gemini and Whisk with Veo 2
source.summary: Transform text-based prompts into high-resolution eight-second videos in Gemini Advanced and use Whisk Animate to turn images into eight-second animated clips.
